narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 77% chance that you would not pass based on your academic and background details. This high predicted risk of failure triggered the rejection to help the university maintain strong bar passage rates for its graduates. The model's decision hinged most heavily on your Year 3 class rank, which fell in the bottom decile—far below the middle-upper deciles typical for students who go on to pass the bar. This low ranking stood out as the strongest signal pushing the prediction toward failure, suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Next in influence was your LSAT score of 24, well under the average of about 37 for bar passers, which further tilted the odds against you by indicating potential hurdles in the rigorous reasoning demands of law school and the bar. Rounding out the top factors was your Year 1 class rank, also in the bottom decile compared to the higher averages of successful students, reinforcing concerns about early academic momentum. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 was somewhat lower than the 3.24 average for passers and your family's low income bracket is less common among them, these played smaller roles here. Your choice to attend full-time aligns with most bar passers, offering a slight positive note amid these concerns. Overall, your profile compared unfavorably to admitted students, whose stronger ranks and test scores typically predict bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted tutoring or additional coursework, and exploring programs with bar prep support or flexible admissions criteria. Building a standout personal statement highlighting your resilience could also help in reapplying elsewhere. With focused improvements, you can shift these predictions in your favor.
